critique.summary: The article introduces Sonar, a command-line interface (CLI) tool designed to identify and manage processes listening on localhost ports. It provides features such as displaying Docker container details, resource usage, killing processes by port, viewing logs, and attaching to services, aiming to simplify port management for developers.
mainPoints: Sonar lists all processes listening on localhost ports, including Docker container names and Compose projects.
It allows users to kill processes, tail logs, shell into containers, and perform other management tasks by specifying port numbers.
The tool supports features like resource usage statistics, health checks, remote scanning via SSH, and port mapping.
Sonar is installable via a shell script and supports shell completions for easier use.
It is compatible with macOS and Linux platforms, using system utilities like lsof and ss.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Sonar's functionality are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository. The described features such as listing ports, showing Docker container details, killing processes by port, and viewing logs align with the documented capabilities of the tool. The installation instructions and supported platforms also match the official documentation. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: The article does not mention potential security implications of using a tool that can kill processes and attach shells remotely, which could be significant in multi-user or production environments.
It does not discuss performance impact or limitations when running Sonar on systems with a large number of ports or containers.
The article lacks information about the tool's maintenance status or community support beyond the GitHub stars and forks.
No comparison with similar existing tools is provided to contextualize Sonar's unique advantages or disadvantages.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a new CLI tool called Sonar designed to identify and kill processes running on localhost ports. Users appreciate its simplicity and usefulness compared to traditional commands like lsof, while suggesting improvements and sharing alternative solutions.

comments.author: raskrebs
comments.text:
I always have a bunch of local projects running, particularly during the weekend where I'm rarely working on one thing at a time. A big pain of mine was constantly running into port: Redis from one project blocking another, orphaned dev servers from old worktrees, Docker containers I forgot about. The usual fix is lsof -iTCP | grep ..., then figuring out what the PID actually is, then killing it. But I always forget the command, and it doesn’t really include all the information that I like.

So I built this lightweight CLI. Single binary, no dependencies. It shows everything listening on localhost with process names, Docker container info, clickable URLs etc.

Sure there are workarounds, but none that satisfied my need for a short, easily rememberable command. Also nothing really has the same satisfaction as running sonar kill 3000 — it just feels nice. I’ve already been approached by a few agent orchestration tools that have been struggling with the same thing. It's really useful when you have multiple agents running, but it's not built for just that use case, I have also find it handy when killing off all containers after a failed cleanup and so on. Also know that MCPs are dead and CLIs are the new thing in agentic coding, this might be a useful tool for Claude, particularly when a compose process exits before all containers are stopped.

Open for contributions, ideas and feedback.
comments.analysis: The author, who is also the creator, provides a detailed and accurate description of the tool's purpose and features, making this the most reliable and informative comment.

comments.author: quotemstr
comments.text:
Christ Almighty I hate our industry practice of binding to some inscrutable port number on localhost. Unix domain sockets aren't that hard! They're secure against all sorts of attacks and more convenient to boot. Instead of connecting to a number, you connect to a file. An ordinary file, with an ordinary name you can mv, chmod, and rm. Boring on a good way.

So why doesn't everyone run local services over Unix sockets?

The only problems: 1) web browsers don't support AF_UNIX URI scheme, and 2) ancient versions of Java don't have built-in APIs for AF_UNIX sockets.

That's it. For these trivial reasons, we've beat our head against arbitrary opaque numbers for decades.

And so, for want of a nail, the Unix was lost.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a thoughtful critique of the common practice of using TCP ports instead of Unix sockets, providing valid technical reasons and limitations, showing good insight.

comments.author: chwzr
comments.analysis: Provides a practical shell function alternative to Sonar, demonstrating equivalent functionality with lsof and kill commands, useful for users reluctant to install new tools.

comments.author: klaushardt
comments.text:
Would be nice to have a flag to customize the URL displayed for Docker containers. I connect to my host via Tailscale, but I can’t open links with localhost. It would be helpful to have a parameter that allows us to choose a network device or specify an IP address to display.

    3000    wud (getwud/wud:latest)                            wud          getwud/wud:latest                       3000    http://localhost:3000
    3001    dockhand (fnsys/dockhand:latest)                   dockhand     fnsys/dockhand:latest                   3000    http://localhost:3001
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion to improve the tool's usability in networked environments like Tailscale, showing practical user perspective.

comments.author: maciejj
comments.text: Nice! I always forget the lsof flags and end up googling them every time. Would be cool if it could run in the system tray and show what's running on your ports at a glance. Also, the name had me thinking SonarQube at first, might be worth considering a rename to avoid the confusion.
comments.analysis: Offers positive feedback and a useful feature suggestion, also noting potential confusion with the SonarQube name, indicating awareness of user experience.

comments.author: kohexo
comments.text: Honestly, pretty cool. I was wondering if something like this existed. Right now I have scripts to kill the ports I use consistently to avoid issues when developing. Kudos!
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and appreciation for the tool, validating its usefulness compared to personal scripts.

comments.author: fcoury
comments.text:
We live in crazy times. I wanted to add a PID to the list for my personal use and since I use Rust way more than Go, I decided to one-shot one app, and Codex indeed one shotted it, wow.

https://github.com/fcoury/sonars
comments.analysis: Shares a personal extension of the tool using Rust, showing engagement and customization, though less detailed about the original tool.

comments.author: pdimitar
comments.text:
I am absolutely installing this and starting to use it daily!

For the even less patient there's also this (not mine): https://github.com/jkfran/killport
comments.analysis: Shows enthusiasm and provides an alternative tool link, useful but less substantive about Sonar itself.

comments.author: mustafa0x
comments.text:
Nice util. It should be possible to kill inline.

I like clack/prompts. See its multiselect API.

https://github.com/bombshell-dev/clack/tree/main/packages/pr...
comments.analysis: Suggests a usability improvement and references a related library, indicating constructive engagement.

comments.author: clutchski
comments.analysis: Mentions an alternative tool, contributing to the discussion but with minimal detail.

comments.author: RonanSoleste
comments.text: I just have an alias in my .bashrc :)
comments.analysis: A brief comment that adds little value to the discussion beyond stating a personal workaround.

comments.author: moezd
comments.text: Sonar as in SonarQube? That's an interesting choice for a name :)
comments.analysis: A mild observation about the tool's name, not contributing to the technical discussion.

comments.author: Bradd3rs
comments.text: love this, i get tired of spamming lsof -i tcp:xxxx
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment and frustration with existing commands, but lacks depth.

comments.author: frankdenbow
comments.analysis: Short positive comment echoing common frustrations, minimal analytical content.

comments.author: jkestner
comments.text: I read the readme. :) Very nice. Thoughtful features. Get this on Homebrew!
comments.analysis: Positive feedback with a suggestion for distribution, but brief and not deeply analytical.
